sections.title: FCA may impose requirements on payment institutions for authorisation
sections.summary: The FCA can include specific requirements in an authorisation for payment institutions. These requirements can mandate actions or prohibitions, and may reference the institution's group members. The FCA may also require establishing a separate corporate entity if the institution engages in non-payment services that risk financial stability or effective supervision. Requirements expire at a specified period and do not affect other authorisation powers.

These conditions directly impact data transfer and processing by requiring specific safeguards for financial integrity.

sections.summary: An authorised payment institution, small payment institution, or registered account information service provider that conducts payment services in the UK without proper FCA authorisation or registration is deemed to have contravened the regulations. This section establishes a contravention threshold for payment service providers operating without regulatory approval. This section defines key terms used in the Payment Services Regulations 2017. It specifies the meaning of 'authorised insurer', 'authorised credit institution', and 'authorised custodian' in the context of payment services. These definitions clarify which entities qualify as authorized under the regulations for specific financial activities.

sections.title: Outsourcing operational functions with third parties
sections.summary: An authorised payment institution must notify the FCA of its intention to outsource operational functions. If outsourcing an important operational function, the institution must ensure it does not impair internal controls, FCA oversight, user obligations, regulatory compliance, or financial soundness. The outsourcing must not delegate responsibility for regulatory compliance to third parties. The institution must also notify the FCA of any changes to outsourced functions.
sections.dtia_summary: Outsourcing operational functions requires specific safeguards to ensure regulatory compliance, internal control integrity, and FCA oversight continuity. This section imposes conditions on how payment institutions can delegate critical functions to third parties without compromising their regulatory standing.

requirements.text: An authorised payment institution must notify the FCA of its intention to enter into a contract with another person under which that other person will carry out any operational function relating to its provision of payment services
Where an authorised payment institution intends to outsource any important operational function, all of the following conditions must be met—
The outsourcing is not undertaken in such a way as to impair the quality of the authorised payment institution's internal control

The outsourcing does not result in any delegation by the senior management of the authorised payment institution of responsibility for complying with the requirements imposed by or under these Regulations
The outsourcing does not impair the ability of the FCA to monitor and retrace the authorised payment institution's compliance with these Regulations
The relationship and obligations of the authorised payment institution towards its payment service users under these Regulations is not substantially altered
Compliance with the conditions which the authorised payment institution must observe in order to be authorised and remain so is not adversely affected
None of the conditions of the payment institution's authorisation requires removal or variation

sections.title: Liability for unauthorized payment transactions
sections.summary: This section defines when a payer is liable for losses from unauthorized payment transactions. The payer is liable if they acted fraudulently or failed to comply with regulation 72 due to gross negligence. The payer is not liable if the loss resulted from undetectable theft, employee misconduct, or notification under regulation 72(1)(b).

sections.title: Cash deposits into payment accounts: immediate availability requirements
sections.summary: Payment service providers must ensure cash deposits from users into their payment accounts are available immediately for consumers, micro-enterprises, and charities. For all other users, funds must be available within one business day after receipt. The value date for the payment must align with the business day when funds are credited to the recipient's account. This section requires payment initiation service providers to compensate account servicing providers for losses from failed transactions where the payment order was not received correctly or authenticated. The compensation applies if the payment initiation service provider fails to prove compliance with receipt and authentication requirements under regulation 81. The compensation must be immediate upon request from the account servicing provider.

sections.title: FCA publication of contravention statements
sections.summary: If the FCA determines a person has violated payment service regulations, it may publish a statement of this contravention. The statement applies to payment service providers, authorised payment institutions, small payment institutions, or registered account information services providers. The FCA must follow specific procedural steps including issuing warning notices and decision notices before taking action.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a process for the FCA to formally document and publish contraventions, which may impact data transfer assessments when regulatory actions affect data handling practices across borders.

The FCA must issue a decision notice to the person concerned without delay after considering representations.

sections.title: Liability for offences committed by corporate entities
sections.summary: This section states that a person convicted of an offence under the Payment Services Regulations 2017 is not liable to financial penalties under regulations 111 or 127 for the same contravention of a requirement imposed by the Regulations. It further specifies that officers of bodies corporate, partnerships, and unincorporated associations can be held liable for offences committed with their consent or neglect, and defines the term 'officer' for different entity types.

requirements.text: A person convicted of an offence under these Regulations is not liable to a penalty under regulation 111 or 127 in respect of the same contravention of a requirement imposed by or under these Regulations

If an offence under these Regulations committed by a body corporate is shown to have been committed with the consent or connivance of an officer, the officer as well as the body corporate is guilty of the offence and liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly

If an offence under these Regulations committed by a body corporate is shown to be attributable to any neglect on their part, the officer as well as the body corporate is guilty of the offence and liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly

If an offence under these Regulations committed by a partnership is shown to have been committed with the consent or connivance of a partner, the partner as well as the partnership is guilty of the offence and liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly

If an offence under these Regulations committed by a partnership is shown to be attributable to any neglect on their part, the partner as well as the partnership is guilty of the offence and liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly

If an offence under these Regulations committed by an unincorporated association (other than a partnership) is shown to have been committed with the consent or connivance of an officer, the officer as well as the association is guilty of the offence and liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly

If an offence under these Regulations committed by an unincorporated association (other than a partnership) is shown to be attributable to any neglect of such officer, the officer as well as the association is guilty of the offence and liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly
In relation to a body corporate, 'officer' means a director, manager, secretary, chief executive, member of the committee of management, or a person purporting to act in such a capacity
In relation to an unincorporated association, 'officer' means any officer of the association or any member of its governing body, or a person purporting to act in such capacity
'Partner' includes a person purporting to act as a partner

sections.heading: In section 108 of the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 (relationship with Part 8 (cont.)
sections.title: Account information services insurance requirements
sections.summary: Electronic money institutions must hold professional indemnity insurance covering territories where they offer account information services and potential liabilities to payment service providers and users from unauthorized access or fraud, up to an amount set by the Financial Conduct Authority. This insurance must cover both the geographic scope of services and financial exposure related to data breaches or fraudulent transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires insurance for account information services to cover financial liability from unauthorized access or fraud, impacting data transfer risk assessments for payment services across borders.

requirements.text: Electronic money institutions must hold professional indemnity insurance covering territories where they offer account information services
Insurance must cover potential liability to payment service providers and users from unauthorized access or fraud
Insurance must cover potential liability up to an amount directed by the Financial Conduct Authority

sections.source_quote: in the case of an applicant which proposes to carry on account information services, it holds professional indemnity insurance or a comparable guarantee, which covers— (i) the territories in which the applicant proposes to offer account information services; and (ii) the applicant's potential liability to account servicing payment service providers and payment service users resulting from unauthorised or fraudulent access to, or use of, payment account information, up to such amount as the Authority may direct.

sections.heading: In section 108 of the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 (relationship with Part 8 (cont.)
sections.title: EEA branch registration and supervision procedures
sections.summary: Authorised electronic money institutions must notify the Financial Conduct Authority of changes to EEA branches, including details of management, organizational structure, and operational functions. The Authority must inform the host state competent authority within one month of receiving a complete notice of intention, and may issue warning notices or decisions on whether to permit passport rights in EEA states based on compliance with money laundering and terrorist financing regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires detailed EEA branch registration and notification procedures that impact cross-border data transfers, including compliance with money laundering regulations and coordination with host state authorities.

requirements.text: Authorised electronic money institutions must notify the Authority of changes to EEA branches including management details and organizational structure
The Authority must inform the host state competent authority within one month of receiving a complete notice of intention
The Authority may issue warning notices if it determines an institution is not permitted to exercise passport rights in an EEA state
The Authority must decide within three months whether to register an EEA branch or cancel existing registration

sections.source_quote: If any of the information provided by an authorised electronic money institution in a notice of intention changes, including by the addition of a further branch, the institution must give the Authority notice of such changes in a further notice of intention. The Authority must, within one month beginning with the date on which it receives a complete notice of intention, inform the host state competent authority of the information contained in the notice of intention.

sections.heading: In section 108 of the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 (relationship with Part 8 (cont.)
sections.title: EEA agent services: host state authority notification and decision timelines
sections.summary: The Authority must consider host state competent authority assessments when deciding on EEA agent service applications. The Authority must notify the host state competent authority of its decision within two months for UK services or three months for EEA agent services, providing reasons if the Authority disagrees with the host state's assessment.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires notification to host state authorities for EEA agent services, with specific timelines for decision communication affecting cross-border data transfer compliance.

requirements.text: Authority must take into account host state competent authority information when making decisions on EEA agent service applications
Authority must notify host state competent authority of decision within two months for UK services
Authority must notify host state competent authority of decision within three months for EEA agent services
Authority must provide reasons for decision if it disagrees with host state competent authority assessment

sections.source_quote: Where the application relates to the provision of services in exercise of passport rights through an EEA agent, the Authority must— (a) take into account any information received from the host state competent authority in making its decision; and (b) notify the host state competent authority of its decision, providing reasons for that decision if the Authority does not agree with the assessment of the host state competent authority.

sections.heading: In section 108 of the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 (relationship with Part 8 (cont.)
sections.title: EEA agent services: notification of service commencement dates
sections.summary: Authorised electronic money institutions must notify the Authority of the date they start providing services in another EEA State through a registered EEA agent. The Authority must then notify the host state competent authority of this date.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires electronic money institutions to disclose service commencement dates in EEA states, facilitating cross-border regulatory coordination.

requirements.text: Authorised electronic money institution must notify Authority of date starting services in another EEA State through registered EEA agent
Authority must notify host state competent authority of service commencement date

sections.source_quote: An authorised electronic money institution must notify the Authority of the date on which it starts to provide services in another EEA State through a registered EEA agent, and the FCA must notify such date to the host state competent authority.

Payment service providers must specify framework contract duration
Payment service users have right to terminate framework contract with specified conditions

sections.source_quote: 6. sections.heading: In the Financial Services and Markets Act 2000 (Regulated Activities) Order 2001 M99—
sections.title: Method A for own funds calculation
sections.summary: Method A calculates the own funds requirement as 10% of the authorized payment institution's fixed overheads for the preceding financial year, with potential adjustments by the FCA if there are material changes in the institution's business.

requirements.text: The own funds requirement is 10% of the authorised payment institution's fixed overheads for the preceding financial year

If a material change has occurred in an authorised payment institution's business since the preceding financial year, the FCA may direct that the own funds requirement is to be a higher or lower amount than that calculated in accordance with sub-paragraph (2)

sections.source_quote: The own funds requirement is 10% of the authorised payment institution's fixed overheads for the preceding financial year.

sections.heading: In regulation 2(1)(c) of the Child Trust Funds Regulations 2004 (interpretation) M100, in the
sections.title: Method B for own funds calculation
sections.summary: Method B calculates the own funds requirement as a weighted sum of payment volumes, with specific percentages applied to different ranges of payment volume. The scaling factor adjusts the requirement based on the payment services provided.

requirements.text: The own funds requirement is the sum of the following elements multiplied by the scaling factor—4% of the first 5,000,000 euros of payment volume; 2.5% of the next 5,000,000 euros of payment volume; 1% of the next 90,000,000 euros of payment volume; 0.5% of the next 150,000,000 euros of payment volume; and 0.25% of any remaining payment volume

The "scaling factor" is 0.5 for a payment institution that is authorised to provide only the payment service specified in paragraph 1(f) of Schedule 1 (money remittance); and 1 for a payment institution that is authorised to provide any other payment service specified in paragraph 1(a) to (e) of Schedule 1

sections.source_quote: The own funds requirement is the sum of the following elements multiplied by the scaling factor—(a) 4% of the first 5,000,000 euros of payment volume; (b) 2.5% of the next 5,000,000 euros of payment volume; (c) 1% of the next 90,000,000 euros of payment volume; (d) 0.5% of the next 150,000,000 euros of payment volume; and (e) 0.25% of any remaining payment volume.

sections.heading: In the Payment to Treasury of Penalties (Enforcement Costs of the Payment Systems
sections.title: Consequence of unauthorised payment services
sections.summary: An authorised payment institution, small payment institution, or registered account information service provider that conducts payment services in the UK without proper FCA authorisation or registration is deemed to have contravened the regulations. This section establishes a contravention threshold for payment service providers operating without regulatory approval. This section defines key terms used in the Payment Services Regulations 2017. It specifies the meaning of 'authorised insurer', 'authorised credit institution', and 'authorised custodian' in the context of payment services. These definitions clarify which entities qualify as authorized under the regulations for specific financial activities.

requirements.text: An authorised payment institution, small payment institution or registered account information service provider that carries on payment services in the UK without FCA authorisation or registration is deemed to have contravened the regulations
An authorised insurer is a person authorised under the 2000 Act to effect insurance contracts as principal
An authorised credit institution accepts deposits or approved foreign credit institutions
An authorised custodian safeguards and administers investments

sections.source_quote: 21. If an authorised payment institution, a small payment institution or a registered account information service provider carries on a payment service in the United Kingdom, or purports to do so, other than in accordance with an authorisation or registration granted to it by the FCA, it is to be taken to have contravened a requirement imposed on it under these Regulations.

In regulation 105 of Money Laundering Regulations 2017, substitute '2009' with '2017' in paragraphs (1) and (2)(c)

sections.source_quote: In the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 M120— (a) in regulation 3(1) (general interpretation), in the definitions of "payment services" and "payment service provider" for "2009" substitute " 2017 " ; (b) in regulation 53 (interpretation), in the definition of "telecommunication, digital and IT payment service provider" for the words from "falling within" to the end substitute " consisting of the execution of payment transactions where the consent of the payer to execute a payment transaction is given by means of any telecommunication, digital or IT device and the payment is made to the telecommunication, IT system or network operator acting only as an intermediary between the payment service user and the supplier of the goods and services (and terms used in this definition which are defined in the Payment Services Regulations 2017 have the meanings given in those Regulations). " ; (c) in regulation 60(5) (cancellation and suspension of registration)— (i) in sub-paragraph (b) for "2009" substitute " 2017 " ; (ii) in sub-paragraph (c)(i)— (aa) for "(c) to (h)" substitute " (d) to (j) " ; (bb) for "2009" substitute " 2017 " ; (iii) in sub-paragraph (c)(ii) for "121" substitute " 154(2) " ; (d) in regulation 62(1) (transfer of funds supervisory authorities), in sub-paragraphs (b) and (c) for "2009" substitute " 2017 " ; (e) in regulation 77(2) (power to impose civil penalties: suspension and removal of authorisation), in paragraphs (a)(ii) and (iii) and (b)(ii) and (iii) for "2009" substitute " 2017 " ; (f) in regulation 105 (disclosure by the Commissioners), in paragraphs (1) and (2)(c) for "2009" substitute " 2017 " .
